result: The wind doesn’t remember your name, but it keeps trying to pronounce it every time it rattles the maple outside the kitchen window. I like to think that’s a kind of loyalty: invisible, unrequested, yet returning nightly to disturb the same three leaves that have refused to fall since November. They are stubborn the way old love letters are stubborn, clinging to their branch with the same glue of maybe and what-if. Inside, the radiator hisses like a tired cat, and I drink cinnamon tea from the mug you cracked the morning we argued about whether dust was mostly skin or mostly star. Both, probably; we were both right and both wrong, the way people always are when they want to be believed more than they want to be kind. I keep meaning to sweep the corners, but the piles remind me we’re still becoming earth, one gray flake at a time. Sometimes I hold my palm above the floorboards and feel the house breathing, a slow lungful that smells of coffee grounds and the ghost of your cedar cologne. If I close my eyes I can almost hear you stacking firewood in the yard, whistling that off-key Dylan song about shelter from the storm, the one you never quite learned past the chorus. The truth is, storms don’t ask for names; they just move through, ripping or caressing, and what they leave is simply more room for wind to practice syllables that will never quite sound like goodbye, only like air learning the shape of what it once held.

They are stubborn the way old love letters are stubborn, clinging to their branch with the same glue of maybe and what-if. Inside, the radiator hisses like a tired cat, and I drink cinnamon tea from the mug you cracked the morning we argued about whether dust was mostly skin or mostly star. Both, probably; we were both right and both wrong, the way people always are when they want to be believed more than they want to be kind. I keep meaning to sweep the corners, but the piles remind me we’re still becoming earth, one gray flake at a time. Sometimes I hold my palm above the floorboards and feel the house breathing, a slow lungful that smells of coffee grounds and the ghost of your cedar cologne. If I close my eyes I can almost hear you stacking firewood in the yard, whistling that off-key Dylan song about shelter from the storm, the one you never quite learned past the chorus. The truth is, storms don’t ask for names; they just move through, ripping or caressing, and what they leave is simply more room for wind to practice syllables that will never quite sound like goodbye, only like air learning the shape of what it once held.
